defect: none. Talos supports SideCopy targeting Indian military ('targeting government personnel in India', decoys 'belonging to the military and think tanks'); high confidence matches; doctrine link is strongly_inferred with attesting null so the KEY RULE does not apply.

Target inflation. Both targets (sectors/civil-society/dissident and sectors/government) are unsupported by the only resolving source. The DOJ release describes targeting 'computers, like mobile phones, around the world, including in the United States' and 'access credentials for online accounts issued by U.S. companies' and never names dissidents/civil-society/government as victims (every 'government' hit is nav-chrome or refers to the U.A.E. client government). The dissident/civil-society profile derives from Reuters Project Raven, but src_1 is a 639-char Wayback nav stub and src_2 is empty.

none material. Attribution 'high' matches 'Talos believes with high confidence that this is the work of the Arid Viper threat actor'; civil-society/dissident targets supported ('activists', Palestinian individuals); no attested doctrine links; start_date matches 'around October 2021'.

KEY RULE violated on two attested doctrine links, both attested to the CISA Log4j advisory (a purely technical mitigation CSA). (1) uk/ncs-2016: the advisory never names NCS 2016 or a 'defend pillar'. (2) uk/five-eyes: the advisory has zero hits for 'Five Eyes'/'FVEY'/'UKUSA' — it only lists partner agencies (ACSC, CCCS, NCSC-UK, etc.). Both attested strategic-goal labels are inferred from the signatory list, not named by the attesting source.

none. Both attested doctrine links are explicitly named in the attesting NSArchive CNMF source ('deployed its largest hunt forward team yet'; 'Defend Forward strategy'). Targets gov+critical-infrastructure (ua) supported; dates confirmed ('December 2021 to March 2022', days before the invasion). Self-acknowledged op; high/concur appropriate.

none. The event is the doctrine publication itself; the attested uk/ncs-2022 link's attesting source is the gov.uk 'National Cyber Strategy 2022' page ('Published 15 December 2021'), so the strategic goal is named by definition. Publication date confirmed by the page's updates log.
none. Low confidence / 'suspected' framing matches source; no doctrine links; source fully supports the event and targets.

KEY RULE: doctrine_link ru/russkiy-mir is confidence='attested' with attesting_source_id mstic/2022-01-15, but that source does not name the strategic goal and does not even attribute to Russia: 'MSTIC has not found any notable associations between this observed activity, tracked as DEV-0586, and other known activity groups' and 'MSTIC is not able to assess intent.' The reasoning's claim that 'MSTIC attribution language frames the operation as Russian state-sponsored' overreaches the cited source. (The Russia/Cadet-Blizzard attribution rests on the 2023 source and is fine.)

(1) The summary and the attested uk/ncs-2022 reasoning frame the advisory as 'the day before the Russian invasion' at 'elevated wartime tempo', but the attesting NCSC source explicitly disclaims this: 'N.B This is a routine advisory and not directly linked to the situation in Ukraine.' (2) KEY RULE: neither source names 'NCS 2022', 'Pillar 5', or 'NCS 2016', so both attested doctrine_links overreach. Sandworm/GRU attribution itself is well supported.

Two source-contradiction defects, both citing eset/2022-02-24. (1) Attribution: record names ru/gru/74455/sandworm at confidence='high'/concur with attribution_source_id = the ESET source, but ESET explicitly does NOT attribute: 'ESET Research has not yet been able to attribute these attacks to a known threat actor' / 'we have not found any tangible connection with a known threat actor.' High-confidence Sandworm rests on Mandiant/CERT-UA, which are NOT among the attached sources. (2) KEY RULE: ru/russkiy-mir is 'attested' against the same ESET source, which never names the goal and does not attribute to Russia.
none. Moderate confidence / 'suspected' matches source; no doctrine links; country null; source fully supports.

KEY RULE: ir/forward-defense is confidence='attested' with attesting_source_id cisa/2022-02-24_aa22-055a, but AA22-055A never names 'forward defense' or any Iranian strategic goal; it only states MOIS tasking and sectors ('part of Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), targeting ... telecommunications, defense, local government, and oil and natural gas'). The reasoning concedes the source only 'characterizes the target set'. MOIS attribution and sectors are otherwise well supported.

none. Source supports every summary fact (Fantasy, Sandals, 12 March 2022, IL/ZA/HK victims, diamond industry, code from Apostle). actor_id ir/mois/agrius is more specific than ESET's 'Iran-aligned group', but the assessment is honestly 'concur-with-caveat' with the caveat recorded. Doctrine link is strongly_inferred/null so the KEY RULE does not apply. Targets match.

Summary asserts 'deploying Windows and Android implants', but the fetched CFR source contains no 'Windows'/'Android'/'implant'; it only says attackers 'used fabricated Facebook profiles to lure targets to install trojanized direct message applications.' The prepass assumed the detail came from the Cybereason 'Read more', but only the link title was fetched, not the Cybereason content. Attribution (moderate/Suspected) and targets are fine.

none. Source supports the event (2020-2021 suspected Pegasus infections at 10 Downing Street and FCO) and targets (gov/civilian, foreign-ministry). attribution actor_id correctly null; assessment honestly notes Citizen Lab names no specific UAE service. Attested ae/procurement-led-capability is defensible: source states the PM's Office infection 'was associated with a Pegasus operator we link to the UAE', directly evidencing UAE use of procured commercial spyware. The other doctrine link is strongly_inferred/null. 'high' confidence is slightly generous vs the source's hedging but the operator-linkage itself is asserted confidently.

Summary impact figure 'an estimated 2 million customers offline' is not in the cited ESET source, which only says 'Scheduled execution of Industroyer2 to cut power in an Ukrainian region' with no customer count. Attribution ('We assess with high confidence that the APT group Sandworm is responsible') and actor_id (GRU Unit 74455) are well supported; attested ru/energy-weaponization is supported by the explicit substation/cut-power targeting, though the reasoning's 'paired with kinetic missile strikes on the same grid' clause is not in this source.

Two of three attributions are unsupported by the cited source. The fetched Microsoft blog names ONLY 'Iridium' (one occurrence): 'attributes wiper malware attacks we previously disclosed to a Russian nation-state actor we call Iridium.' The records for ru/gru/26165/apt28 and ru/svr/apt29 are both confidence='high' with attribution_source_id = this blog, yet it never mentions APT28, APT29, Strontium, Nobelium, Fancy Bear or Cozy Bear — an attribution_source_id/source mismatch. The prepass itself rated attribution support 'partial'/'unverifiable' but still stamped clean. Secondary: attested ru/military-doctrine-2014 labels the goal 'non-contact warfare / 2014 military doctrine', a term the source does not use ('hybrid war').

none. start_date matches 'In January 2022 ... South Korea'; disclosure matches blog date 14 Apr 2022; 'high' matches 'North Korea-linked ... Lazarus'; targets (chemical + IT, KR) match 'mainly in the chemical sector, with some being in the information technology (IT) sector ... South Korea'. Doctrine is strongly_inferred/null (KEY RULE n/a).

Date not supported by source. disclosure_date is 2022-08-04 but the only date in the source is the byline '11 Aug 2022' — disclosure_date predates the source's own publication by 7 days. start_date 2022-03-01 has no support in the fetched text (the only date-like string '2022/07' is a malware expiry-config value, not the campaign timeline). Attribution/targets/doctrine otherwise sound.

none. Attested ae/procurement-led-capability is justified: CyberScoop quotes the ITAR charges that the men provided 'defense services involving electronic systems, equipment, and software ... specially designed for intelligence purposes ... to foreign persons in the United Arab Emirates', naming UAE-government acquisition via contractors. Attribution names Adams/Baier/Gericke, Project Raven, DarkMatter. attributing_org is 'US Department of State' while the resolved source is CyberScoop (secondary), but the DOJ DPA primary corroborates.

none. Attested us/hunt-forward is gold-standard: CYBERCOM source explicitly names the 'first defensive Hunt Operation in Albania' / 'first-ever defensive cyber operation there'; goal named, officially acknowledged. Targets sectors/government (AL) confirmed. us/ndaa-1642 is strongly_inferred/null (KEY RULE n/a). CISA AA22-264A corroborates the Iranian-actor attacks.

none. Dates match ('late April to mid-September 2022'; blog Sept 29 2022). 'high' matches 'MSTIC attributes this campaign with high confidence to ZINC, a state-sponsored group based out of North Korea'. Targets (defense + IT/managed-services) match 'media, defense and aerospace, and IT services'. Doctrine strongly_inferred/null (KEY RULE n/a).
